items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to Berlin' (by train) in German?
items.why: Students use gehen for any 'going'. In German, gehen = go ON FOOT only. FAHREN = go by vehicle (car, train, bus). FLIEGEN = go by plane. To Berlin by train: ich fahre. Saying 'ich gehe nach Berlin' suggests walking there!

items.q: How do you say 'I drove to Munich' in the perfect tense?
items.why: Students use haben. Verbs of MOTION (fahren, gehen, kommen, fliegen, reisen) and CHANGE OF STATE take SEIN in the perfect. Critical for holiday vocab. 'Ich bin gegangen', 'ich bin geflogen', 'ich bin gefahren'.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm travelling to Germany' in German?
items.why: Students use 'in' or 'zu'. For most countries and cities: NACH (Deutschland, Berlin, Paris). For countries with articles (die Schweiz, die USA): IN with accusative. Easy to mix up - nach is for proper place names without articles.
Correct answer: Ich bin nach Berlin gefahren. “Ich habe nach Berlin gefahren” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's besucht. “gebesucht” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many irregular past participles are listed in the walkthrough (gefahren, geflogen, gegessen, gesehen)?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
